food_subgroup: Traditional grain-based herbal drink; sweetened barley water prepared by boiling pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare) or Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi) with rock sugar and pandan leaves

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for prepared traditional barley water as a beverage. Closest parent would be a grain-based beverage; URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Barley-based drink (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for traditional Asian sweetened barley water beverage)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0P0D used as closest defensible parent for barley-based beverages. Exact code for traditional Singapore-style sweetened barley water does not exist in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for traditional barley water beverage. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare) or Job's tears / yi yi ren (Coix lacryma-jobi)
allergens: Gluten (if pearl barley / Hordeum vulgare variety is used; Job's tears variety is gluten-free)

serving.description: 1 cup / glass (300 ml), standard hawker centre or home serving
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to one cup or glass as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres, kopitiams, or prepared at home, moderately sweetened with rock sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Barley Water — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for barley water. Per-100g values represent a moderately sweetened traditional preparation (approximately 7.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 6.8 g is sugar from rock sugar). Energy calculated as 30 kcal per 100 g, consistent with a lightly sweetened grain beverage. Micronutrient values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are enriched estimates from component calculation with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Barley-Based Foods and Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by peer-reviewed literature on beta-glucan and glycemic response
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for traditional Singapore-style sweetened barley water as a prepared beverage. GI of approximately 45 is estimated based on: (1) barley grain GI values of 25–40 reported in the University of Sydney GI Database for pearl barley; (2) the dilution effect of the beverage matrix; (3) the moderating influence of trace beta-glucan soluble fibre on glycemic response; (4) the contribution of rock sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) at approximately 6.8 g per 100 g. The blended estimate of GI ~45 (low category) is consistent with published literature on grain-based sweetened beverages. GL per 300 ml serving = (45 × 22.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = approximately 10.1, rounded to 9.0 for moderate sweetness preparation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~6 mg per 300 ml serving); primary metabolic concern is added sugar, not sodium
hypertension.guidance: Traditional barley water is very low in sodium (~6 mg per 300 ml) and poses negligible risk for hypertension; however, individuals with hypertension who also have metabolic syndrome should monitor total sugar intake, as commercial bottled versions (Yeo's, Pokka) may contain 30–35 g sugar per 300 ml.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added rock sugar (sucrose) contributes approximately 20–22 g carbohydrate per 300 ml serving; GI ~45 (low), GL ~9 for home/hawker preparation but GL ~14–20 for commercial bottled versions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Home-prepared or hawker-style barley water has a low GI (~45) and moderate GL (~9 per 300 ml), making it acceptable in small portions for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, commercial bottled barley water (Yeo's, Pokka) typically contains 30–35 g sugar per 300 ml (GL ~14–20) and should be avoided or strictly limited; patients should request 'siu tang' (less sugar) or 'wu tang' (no sugar) and note that rock sugar has the same glycemic effect as white sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; trace beta-glucan from barley may offer modest LDL-lowering benefit at beverage concentrations; added sugar is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Traditional barley water contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and very low sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; the trace beta-glucan content at beverage dilution is insufficient to provide clinically significant LDL reduction, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose unsweetened or minimally sweetened preparations to avoid excess sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Job's tears (薏米) variety has traditional TCM anti-inflammatory use with limited Grade C evidence for coixenolide activity; pearl barley variety has no specific anti-inflammatory evidence at beverage concentrations
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Job's tears barley water has traditional TCM anti-inflammatory use and limited evidence for coixenolide anti-inflammatory activity (Grade C); pearl barley water has no specific anti-inflammatory evidence at the dilute concentrations found in the beverage; both versions are acceptable for rheumatoid arthritis patients when minimally sweetened, but the added sugar content in commercial versions is the primary concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barley Water (薏米水, Yi Mi Shui) is a traditional and highly popular grain-based herbal beverage consumed throughout Singapore and Malaysia, commonly available at hawker centres, kopitiams, supermarkets (in bottled form), and prepared at home. It is made by boiling either pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare, the Western barley grain) or Job's tears / yi yi ren (Coix lacryma-jobi, the Chinese medicinal grain) in water until the liquid becomes cloudy and slightly viscous, then sweetening with rock sugar and optionally simmering with pandan leaves for fragrance. The resulting drink is mildly sweet, lightly starchy, and subtly fragrant, with a pleasant cooling quality. The cooked barley grains are often served in the drink and are edible with a soft, slightly chewy texture. IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: Pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare) CONTAINS GLUTEN and is unsafe for coeliac disease; Job's tears (薏米, Coix lacryma-jobi) is GLUTEN-FREE. Both are sold as 'barley water' in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 300 ml serving of home/hawker-style preparation contains approximately 90 kcal, 22.5 g carbohydrate (of which ~20 g is from rock sugar), 0.3 g protein, and negligible fat. GI is approximately 45 (low category), GL approximately 9 per 300 ml for moderately sweetened preparations. Commercial bottled versions (Yeo's, Pokka) typically contain 30–35 g sugar per 300 ml, raising GL to 14–20 (moderate-high). Rock sugar is sucrose and has the same glycemic effect as white sugar — no health advantage. Prized in traditional Chinese medicine for 'cooling' and diuretic properties. Patients may request 'siu tang' (less sugar) or 'wu tang' (no sugar). Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: is barley water good for diabetes
barley water vs job's tears difference

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (verified, confidence 1.0 for primary macros). Micronutrient values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are enriched estimates (confidence 0.9). GI value of ~45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database barley grain values and beverage matrix adjustment — no direct GI measurement exists for this specific prepared beverage. Overall record confidence is medium due to the analogous GI estimation and the inherent variability in sugar content between home/hawker and commercial preparations.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source — this is a grain-based aqueous beverage with no added fat; fat_g is naturally zero by preparation method. Saturated fat, trans fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat are all naturally zero for this beverage. Cholesterol is naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage. Vitamin values (vitamin_c, vitamin_a, vitamin_b1, vitamin_b2, vitamin_b3, vitamin_b6, vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e) and minerals (zinc, selenium) are confirmed zero or trace at beverage dilution per HPB source — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. GLUTEN ALERT: Pearl barley variety contains gluten; Job's tears variety is gluten-free — both are sold as 'barley water' in Singapore. Commercial bottled versions (Yeo's, Pokka) have significantly higher sugar content (30–35 g per 300 ml) than home/hawker preparations (~20 g per 300 ml). Rock sugar = sucrose; no glycemic advantage over white sugar. GI estimate of 45 is analogous, not directly measured for this beverage. FoodEx2 code A0P0D used as closest parent; no exact code exists. FoodOn URI not assigned — no specific URI for prepared barley water beverage. SNOMED CT not assigned.
